issue_comment_body: Please, upgrade pip.

Sorry, I was a bit irritated when I wrote the intial bug-report.

I'll spend some time moving stuff about, and renaming the manifold object, and get something together in the next few days.

Re: The global thing: You are correct, it's not global. I was a bit confused by the fact that the manifold class definition is assigned as a class member, and then the class member class definition is instantiated (this is presumably to allow monkey-patching the manifold object).

Well, it's scoped to `Transactions` by default but that's easily fixable with a little monkeypatch. I believe this should do the trick:
```
from mt940 import tags
from mt940 import models

tags.BalanceBase.scope = models.Transaction
```

Hey Alessandro, thanks for the feedback :)

I have moved FileInfo to FileStorage._FileInfo and kept FileStorage.fileinfo to not break backwards compatibility. Now fileinfo just has extra parameter to be used in "replace" cases. I am open to refactoring _FileInfo.

I agree with you - it is better not to put so many changes in single pull request. If you wish I can rollback "unknown" => "unnamed" renaming and do it in separate pull request.
Now that we have the table, I'm going to go ahead and turn the table names into headings. This will allow the names to show up in the TOC in the sidebar.

Thanks for this contribution! :smiley: I hope I did not overwhelm you with the change requests. But maintaining a clean and consistent API is the most important thing for easy usage in my opinion.

I wonder if we should really have official support for this. It requires additional circuits and is mostly a documentation problem. Especially now that we have I2C support (#34). An I2C port expander board is really cheap (can be found for <1$) and makes handling of backlight control as well as wiring *much* simpler - 4 wires is all that you need. There are even a lot of LCDs with those "backpack boards" already soldered on.

I'll close this for now, feel free to comment if you disagree.

This seems to be a problem with the khronos website (unrelated to glad's code). Downloadcode seems to work sporadically, also I have problems loading the API files and their main website in my browser as well.

Workaround: download the files manually and place them in the same directory as `main.py`, the local files will be used instead of downloading them. (You might need to download [khrplatform.h](https://www.khronos.org/registry/egl/api/KHR/khrplatform.h) and place it in `include/KHR/` (create the directory if it doesn't exist already).

Think i've done it. I made this change to setup.py:

```
# Specify extra link arguments for Windows.

if sys.version.find('MSC') == -1:
    #my_extra_link_args = None
    my_extra_link_args = ["-mmacosx-version-min=10.12"]  # local change jeffs mac
else:
    my_extra_link_args = ["/MANIFEST"]

```

It made the clang error and all the 'was built for newer OSX version (10.12) than being linked (10.6)' warnings go away. Nice.

We discuss this in the past and the problem is that if you reject values based on their type you cannot really interface with user defined types (such as a new numerical type or a different type of iterable to build an array). You would require the user to register it. We have decided to follow the "consenting adults" guidelines.

It's not obvious what the problem is here, but problems often arise from byteswapping in place (e.g., if you have any other references to `data`, they will be turned into nonsense arrays). Try doing
```
datasw = data.byteswap(False).newbyteorder()
```

So uptime in specific, I'd consider either pagerduty (which will has pretty awesome built-in-support), or re-architecting.

Thanks for this detailed report and question.  You're completely right - as is, the roster can get stale.

If we can come up with an elegant solve, I'm all for it.

A couple quick answers to your questions:

- The persisting to redis is for thread-share purposes, and we will need a cross-thread way to persist the data.  At present, that's using redis (or one of the new datastore backends)
- Clearing on startup is possible, but has two problems: 1) We're rate-limited by hipchat, and getting user info ends up being one of the more expensive operations. 2) This will still mean that the roster is stale for orgs that don't restart their wills very often.

Apparently this issue is related to [`pyexpat`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pyexpat.html), which cannot handle python unicode string (at list in python 2.7) ([reference](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-list/2001-December/070358.html)).

I have performed the following test:

- Check python version
```shell
python -V
# => Python 2.7.6
```

- Create a file `amodule.yang`
```yang
module amodule {
  namespace "urn:test:amodule";
  prefix "amodule";

  organization "organização güi";
  contact "àéïç¢ô";

  grouping x {
    leaf y { type string; }
  }

  rpc run {
    input { uses x; }
    output { uses x; }
  }
}
```

- Ensure `pyang -f tree amodule.yang` does not result in error

- Convert it to yin
```shell
pyang -f yin amodule.yang -o amodule.yin
```

- Try to use yin as input
```shell
pyang -f tree amodule.yin
# =>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/pyang", line 434, in <module>
    run()
  File "/usr/local/bin/pyang", line 298, in run
    expect_failure_error=False)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyang/__init__.py", line 72, in add_module
    module = p.parse(self, ref, text)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyang/yin_parser.py", line 88, in parse
    self.parser.Parse(text, True)
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 255-256: ordinal not in range(128)
```

- Test the expat xml parser from interactive python:
```python
from xml.parsers import expat
import io
fp = io.open('amodule.yin', encoding='utf-8')
text = fp.read()
parser = expat.ParserCreate('UTF-8')
parser.Parse(text, True)
# =>
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "<input>", line 1, in <module>
#     parser.Parse(text)
# UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 255-256: ordinal not in range(128)
```

In my point of view, this test is related to the @xianglisegue description in the following sense:

- when `pyang` resolves `import` statements, `yin` files take precedence over `yang` files
- `xml.parsers.expat` (used by `pyang`) cannot parse unicode strings (at least in python 2.7) and results in an `UnicodeEncodeError`
- If no `yin` file is present, the `import` statement is resolved using UTF-8 `yang` file, which `pyang` has no problems in parsing

A workaround could be converting unicode string to ascii, but this change will remove complex characters.
```python
ascii_text = text.encode('ascii', 'ignore')
self.parser.Parse(ascii_text, True)
```

I agree that having one to track business entities in countries would be useful. Regarding the approach, I don't think it's important to keep the original default namespace. In fact, it's not important to maintain the origin prefix definitions at all. As long as the qualified names point to the same URIs, you can redefine the prefixes anyway you like.

For example, ProvToolbox just create new prefix when there is a collision (see the [register() method](https://github.com/lucmoreau/ProvToolbox/blob/master/prov-model/src/main/java/org/openprovenance/prov/model/Namespace.java#L121)).

In our library, the class `NamespaceManager` also does something similar.

So I think you can just register any namespace defined at an Element. However, if the `prov:id` does not have a prefix, I suggest you create the full URI and try generate a qualified name from it.

Accessing fields works slightly differently when using JSON, because of the way the tshark JSON is organized. Fields will be accessed with "." instead of "_" like in wireshark itself (you can see the field name in the bottom left).

Also, you can access a field's value by accessing its name and access its subfields by accessing "<field_name>_tree". That's just the way the JSON is structured.

You can access the field you want by using `packet.wlan.fc_tree.flags_tree.retry`. Or the whole flags field by using `w.wlan.fc_tree.flags`. I recommend using an interpreter to find the attributes you need or just printing the packet to see how to access it.

There's sort of three levels to the code involved when a bot is built with discord.py:
1. Low Level - Raw API requests
2. Mid Level - Mapping the API to appropriate objects, functions and methods via d.py
3. High Level - Your own code on top of the d.py interface

Most implementation details for the higher end stuff is left out of the lib due to a number of reasons, a major one being that the lib is not meant to limit how you implement your own bots higher-end features; how you would prefer to implement one feature for your own bots code could be different to someone else's due to style preference, experience, existing dependencies, performance requirements, rigidity/flexibility and overall user experience.

Asking for something like the above is far too high-level for the lib to care about or get involved in for the most part. It would require a bunch of objects for tracking interactive sessions, building embed paginators, input handling, available choices and their parsing, designed user limitations and other things that I'm sure other will want to control, and it would have to be designed in a way that ensured it won't impact negatively systems that don't have much wiggle room performance-wise, yet somehow be flexible and powerful enough to be modified in almost any way to fulfil the intentions of most users. 

Instead of bloating the lib with unnecessary high-level code that mostly comes down to personal choice of implementation details, you should implement your own preferred setup and work with it. The lib should be separate from that, as it's for all intents, as bare as it should be when it comes to excess features.

Consider even that the commands extension, something used by the majority of regulars in one way or another, is kept separate intentionally from the lib.

AH HA! I figured out why the channel is `None`! For some reason the `on_ready` event is not being fired.

This is caused by the video URL used being old or invalid; the web player fetches a new one every time you open the video so this isn't an issue whereas your bots are pretending to be YouTube and don't.

One solution is store the URL of the video instead of downloading info, and download the info either shortly before the next track is to play (pipelining) or to download the info as soon as the next track plays. This ensures that the URL is always fresh when playing.

The other solution is to download the entire video data, which avoids having to rely on the links not being stale.

I believe I've found out what the real issue is. I'm using Visual Studio 2015 (v14) but the Cython was compiled by setuptools was using VC9.0 for Python. I've googled and found the following comment from one of the Google Groups, https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/cython-users/NYjxdX2UA8I:
"Setuptools is running Visual Studio 9.0, but you have for some reason 
inserted VS 14.0's include dir into the list. Trying to change 
Setuptools to use VS 14.0 is a nightmare that probably won't end, even 
when you rebuild Python itself and all of the packages you're using with 
VS 14.0 also. 

VS 9.0 is required to build Python extensions for Python 2.7 to make 
sure it uses the same version of MSVCRT (to Linux folks, it is basically 
libc) for the extensions as Python itself uses. Mismatched versions of 
MSVCRT can lead to problems caused by each version of MSVCRT using its 
own malloc heap, stdio buffers, locale settings, et cetera. Usually 
you're stopped right away with a DLL load error."

There *has* to be a better way. Maybe we can separate the snd-call from the data conversion/normalization somehow. Maybe we can pass in functions. But reading and writing are such fundamentally different operations, they *should not* live in the same function.

Oh wow, ok.  I mean, I would argue that a PPQ of 32767 is the actual bug.  Yeah, in that case that is just about 3 minutes (which is still a really large spacing between events).  The issue is that `pretty_midi` keeps a cache of tick-to-time conversion, and if ticks like 14325216 are allowed, then the array can get very large (at least 100 megabytes in this case).  The cached tick-to-time array is really a requirement, else a lot of operations would be extremely slow.  The best solution may be to allow the user to override the MAX_TICK setting in a non-hacky way; of course it's totally possible currently like this:

Fixed it by using PyObjC package to obtain NSView pointer:

```
    def get_window_handle(self):
        if self.winfo_id() > 0:
            return self.winfo_id()
        elif MAC:
            # On Mac window id is an invalid negative value (Issue #308).
            # This is kind of a dirty hack to get window handle using
            # PyObjC package. If you change structure of windows then you
            # need to do modifications here as well.
            # noinspection PyUnresolvedReferences
            from AppKit import NSApp
            # noinspection PyUnresolvedReferences
            import objc
            # Sometimes there is more than one window, when application
            # didn't close cleanly last time Python displays an NSAlert
            # window asking whether to Reopen that window.
            # noinspection PyUnresolvedReferences
            return objc.pyobjc_id(NSApp.windows()[-1].contentView())
        else:
            raise Exception("Couldn't obtain window handle")
```

Hi Filip, I really _would_ like to write a regression test, I just need some help figuring out what it should test..

Can we agree that these are Unicode strings (due to `from __future__ import unicode_literals` at the top of the file)? (note: this code was not present in v0.4)

    [('HGENCODING', 'utf-8')]

my contention is that the reason this works on Linux is due to a bug in Python where it passes its Unicode buffer (internally encoded as utf-8?) directly to Linux' equivalent of `CreateProcess`. Since there are no code points above 127 in those strings, this (purely by accident) does not crash.

Python/Linux does not support putting Unicode directly into the environment as witnessed by the following command:

    python -c "import subprocess;subprocess.call('exit 42', shell=True, env={u'æ':u'æ'})"

which on my linux machine produces:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 493, in call
        return Popen(*popenargs, **kwargs).wait()
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 679, in __init__
        errread, errwrite)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 1249, in _execute_child
        raise child_exception
    UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 0-1: ordinal not in range(128)
    vagrant@precise64:~$

meaning that it is expecting ascii.

IOW, the `env` parameter is expecting a dict containing byte-strings and it is being passed two Unicode strings.

On Windows there is a type error regardless of the content of the Unicode strings (even empty strings cause a type error first):

    (dev) go|c:\srv\bin\serverinit> python -c "import subprocess;subprocess.call('exit 42', shell=True, env={u'':u''})"
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "c:\python27\Lib\subprocess.py", line 493, in call
        return Popen(*popenargs, **kwargs).wait()
      File "c:\python27\Lib\subprocess.py", line 679, in __init__
        errread, errwrite)
      File "c:\python27\Lib\subprocess.py", line 896, in _execute_child
        startupinfo)
    TypeError: environment can only contain strings

This is the reason `bumpversion` as-is, currently does not run on Windows (and of course neither does the tests).  I'm assuming the reason the tests ran in version 0.4 was because the offending code (`[('HGENCODING', 'utf-8')]`) did not exist in the code or the tests at that time.

Normally, when I think of regression tests, they are tests using the software and causing a failure before a fix and no failure after a fix.  I hope I've convinced you that this is a real problem on Windows, yet there is no way (as far as _I_ can see) to trigger this problem on Linux, at least not by writing a test that uses any of the code in `bumpversion`. Am I correct?  If so, what would be an acceptable regression test for this change?

It's been a more complicated project.
It mainly consists of a headless virtual machine in which screenshots from several webpages are taken on a regular basis. This is done via "ghost" which is a python module. On a headless machine you need Xvcb running to be able to run a browser and take screenshots.
These screenshots are then cropped and stitched together, also with a python module "PIP".
The stitched image is saved into the root directory of a local webserver. 

The url of this image can then be sent to chromecast using pychromecast.

All the scripts need to be called e.g. every minute via a cronjob to get regular updates on the screen.

In my latest implementation I have skipped the stiching and built the infoscreen layout with the FTUI of the open source home automation FHEM. I therefore now only take one screenshot of this layout and copy it to the webserver to stream it to chromecast.

There are of course many ways to build the image, but to automate the streaming, I haven't found a better way than via ghost and Xvcb.
All security settings should allow it, and I tried using multiple computers.

FETCH is one of the IMAP commands that could be run asynchronously. But you cannot run same commands at the same time. As indicated in the README (IMAP commands concurrency) : 

> if an async command is running, same async commands (or with the same untagged response type) must wait

It is not truly asynchronous execution as the whole is monothreaded and on the same socket. It is *asynchronous on IO*. I don't see why you would run these fetches in parallel. This would have an interest if the fetch could take a while *on the server side*. 

If you really want to run the fetches in parallel, run your fetch command  in different threads but each imap client needs to be used in only one thread.

Update:

I have been doing further investigation on the issue above and have found out some interesting, and helpful (hopefully) things.  I believe the problem discussed above is due to the heading reported by the humminbird unit.  When I chose to calculate the bearing from the coordinates (calc_bearing = 1), I was getting very strange and obviously wrong headings.  After reviewing the rawdata.csv, I found that many of the headings had a value of 0, 90, 330, etc which are clearly not accurate.  After reviewing _pyhum_read, I moved the call for calculating the heading (line 416) to line 437 after the conversion and projection of the northing and easting to latitude and longitude.  See snipet below:

```
    metadat['instr_heading'] = metadat['heading'][:nrec]


    #Move this to line 437
    ##metadat['heading'] = humutils.get_bearing(calc_bearing, filt_bearing, cog, metadat['lat'], metadat['lon'], metadat['instr_heading'])

    try:
       es = humutils.runningMeanFast(metadat['e'][:nrec],len(metadat['e'][:nrec])/100)
       ns = humutils.runningMeanFast(metadat['n'][:nrec],len(metadat['n'][:nrec])/100)
    except:
       es = metadat['e'][:nrec]
       ns = metadat['n'][:nrec]

    metadat['es'] = es
    metadat['ns'] = ns

    try:
       trans =  pyproj.Proj(init=cs2cs_args)
    except:
       trans =  pyproj.Proj(cs2cs_args.lstrip(), inverse=True)

    lon, lat = trans(es, ns, inverse=True)
    metadat['lon'] = lon
    metadat['lat'] = lat


    #New location for calculating the bearing from coordinates
    metadat['heading'] = humutils.get_bearing(calc_bearing, filt_bearing, cog, metadat['lat'], metadat['lon'], metadat['instr_heading'])

    dist_m = humutils.get_dist(lat, lon)
    metadat['dist_m'] = dist_m
```

This resulted in the rawdata.csv containing actual headings.  After pulling the rawdata.csv file and looking at the headings, I found that they weren't perfectly aligned with the trackline.  After investigating the bearingBetweenPoints definition in the utils.py script, I found the error.  Before fix:

```
def bearingBetweenPoints(pos1_lat, pos2_lat, pos1_lon, pos2_lon):
   lat1 = np.deg2rad(pos1_lat)
   lon1 = np.deg2rad(pos1_lon)
   lat2 = np.deg2rad(pos2_lat)
   lon2 = np.deg2rad(pos2_lon)

   bearing = np.arctan2(np.cos(lat1) * np.sin(lat2) - np.sin(lat1) * np.cos(lat2) * np.cos(lon2 - lon1), np.sin(lon2 - lon1) * np.cos(lat2))


   db = np.rad2deg(bearing)
   return (90.0 - db + 360.0) % 360.0
```

After fix:
```

def bearingBetweenPoints(pos1_lat, pos2_lat, pos1_lon, pos2_lon):
   lat1 = np.deg2rad(pos1_lat)
   #lon1 = np.deg2rad(pos1_lon)
   lat2 = np.deg2rad(pos2_lat)
   #lon2 = np.deg2rad(pos2_lon)

   #bearing = np.arctan2(np.cos(lat1) * np.sin(lat2) - np.sin(lat1) * np.cos(lat2) * np.cos(lon2 - lon1), np.sin(lon2 - lon1) * np.cos(lat2))

   diffLong = np.deg2rad(pos2_lon - pos1_lon)
   bearing = np.arctan2(np.cos(lat1) * np.sin(lat2) - (np.sin(lat1) * np.cos(lat2) * np.cos(diffLong)), np.sin(diffLong) * np.cos(lat2))


   db = np.rad2deg(bearing)
   return (90.0 - db + 360.0) % 360.0
```

This is according to [this post](https://gist.github.com/jeromer/2005586).  The longitudes of both points need to be subtracted from each other in degrees before converting to radians.

I believe these two fixes have helped my data rectify correctly, but I haven't fully tested yet.  I will post results later.

Indeed, a raw handler as pointed out in your example can provide you the lowest latency. First you actually need to get started timestamping your data (e.g. data comes, and append in new queue as timestamp and data pairs).

But in your application I'm assuming you need to lower the timing jitter, some thoughts:

* My rough testing and some users reports about speed is that we can cope to similar to native OS speed, so I think python and the data processing is not adding that much overhead.

* Data is collected by a thread using overlapped reading (wait for even), but quickly posted into the device reading thread.

* The device reading thread calls your raw handler, but also would parse HID reports.

So first, just add a timestamp to your incoming data in your raw handler, if this is not enough, go ahead and test disabling the HID report handling, if this is still not enough then try to add the timestamping in the reading queue incoming data handling.

Actually after some more thinking and playing with the implementation, I agree that ``copy_function`` and ``nested`` arguments are not needed. I left only one argument ``dict_factory`` (and ``tuple_factory`` for ``astuple``). As for custom copy function, this can be fixed by using ``__copy__`` in many cases and for shallow ``dict`` one can use ``vars``. For factory however, there are three arguments to keep it:
* There are to many options: ``dict``, ``OrderedDict``, ``SortedDict``, named tuples, etc.
* It is very hard to insert such factory from the user side, if we don't have it as an argument.
* ``attrs`` have the same parameter, so it will be familiar for ``attrs`` users.

Currently the logic is easy to explain in one sentence: make a _deep_ copy replacing dataclasses with ``dict_factory``. The additional bonus is that implementation is very simple. It is pushed in a new commit. Please review.

I've been thinking about how I would implement this feature for a few weeks, here's some things I would consider:

* Like Django does, I would treat the PostGIS implementation as the "gold standard" for boundary handling. For instance, PostGIS supports MultiPolygon fields (eg: polygons with holes) but vanilla PostgreSQL only supports simple Polygon fields (eg: polygons *without* holes). PostGIS seems to be the most featureful and stable GIS-enabled database extension.
* If at all possible, try to also support Spatialite databases. I'm not too worried about supporting MySQL database variants, because most users probably aren't using MySQL for GIS queries to begin with.
* Add the boundary field to the `Place` abstract model, that way all subclasses (which is every model but `AlternativeName`) automatically get boundaries with consistent semantics.
* Adding the boundary fields is probably the easy part. The more difficult part to write is the import script. I'd like to allow users to specify exactly which countries they want to import data from, since the import script already takes a long time to complete.
* Keep in mind that some users may not run the migration/s that add boundary fields to models before they run the import script, so check for the boundary field on the model before trying to import it. See [this line](https://github.com/coderholic/django-cities/blob/master/cities/management/commands/cities.py#L578) for how I handle district models without a `code` field.
* Keep the boundary field nullable, as I don't want to force users with existing data to import boundaries during migrations (I ran into this issue when I added slugs). Migrations to me should only change the database schema, not run a huge import script with a lot of potential to fail in various ways.
* In the import script, I think would implement importing boundaries as it's own option (eg: `python manage.py cities --import=boundary`), and I would *not* import boundaries in the `--import=all` option. This caveat will need to be documented.
* Please include test/s for the import script with a minimum of boundary data (see [this file with cherry picked test data](https://github.com/coderholic/django-cities/blob/master/test_project/data/admin1CodesASCII.txt). It doesn't need to be an in-depth test, something like this would be fine:
  ```python
  class TestImportBoundaryCommand(TestCase):
      def test_import_boundary(self):
          # Before we import boundaries, check that we have none
          self.assertEqual(Country.objects.exclude(boundary=None).count(), 0)

          ... # Call import boundary command

          # Check that we successfully imported some boundaries, even though we really don't care what boundaries we imported
          self.assertGreaterThan(Country.objects.exclude(boundary=None).count(), 0)
  ```
* I would suggest using GEOMETRY datatypes instead of GEOGRAPHY datatypes because I'd like this project to apply worldwide. I would rather this project store data without the need for projections even if it means there's a performance hit. See [this section](http://postgis.net/docs/using_postgis_dbmanagement.html#PostGIS_GeographyVSGeometry) of the PostGIS documentation for details.
* See the [PostGIS overview of datatypes](http://postgis.net/docs/using_postgis_dbmanagement.html#PostGIS_Geography) to figure out which one to pick. Specifically, I don't know if a simple `Polygon` field will work or if a `MultiPolygon` field would be better. The [built-in GIS fields](https://django.readthedocs.io/en/stable/ref/contrib/gis/model-api.html?highlight=string) all correspond to GIS field types, so check out the the polygon vs. multi-polygon examples in [this section](http://postgis.net/docs/using_postgis_dbmanagement.html#OGC_Validity) of the PostGIS documentation for details.
* Do you want to support historical boundaries? If yes, I would suggest utilizing [django-field-history](https://github.com/grantmcconnaughey/django-field-history) on the boundary field. Doing so will automatically save a revision to modified boundaries every time the import command is run, and if the boundary isn't modified it won't save a revision.
* If you do want to save revisions, saving revisions to *just* the boundary field is much preferable to saving revisions of the whole model.

Don't let any of these confuse you or frighten you off - deal with them as issues come up. And feel free to ping me if you have any questions or anything.

Hi selonov,

`Configuration` takes either a `service_endpoint` or a `server` keyword argument. The former is the full URL to the EWS service endpoint, i.e. `https://mail.example.com/EWS/Exchange.asmx`. The latter is the server hostname, i.e. `https://mail.example.com`.

Just use the `server` argument unless your EWS service endpoint is located at a non-standard URL.

Basically "select timeout" means Ubuntu in the VM isn't able to access your virtual webcam provided by VMware Workstation.

First, make sure you've gone into the VMware Workstation settings and enabled your Webcam so that the virtual machine has access to it. Make sure VMware is actually about to get a picture from your webcam.

After that, you might try the suggestion in this post inside of the virtual machine: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12715209/select-timeout-error-in-ubuntu-opencv

here's my solution, seems to be working fine:

`
import os

known_face_names = []
known_face_encodings = []
images = []
i = 0
source = './known_images'
for root, dirs, filenames in os.walk(source):
    for f in filenames:
        picture = (os.path.splitext(f))[0]
        known_face_names.append(picture)
        known_face_encodings.append('{}_face_encoding'.format(picture))
        images.append('{}_image'.format(picture))

        images[i] = face_recognition.load_image_file(source+"/{}.jpg".format(picture))
        known_face_encodings[i] = face_recognition.face_encodings(images[i])[0]

        i += 1
`

The error isn't related to `libpng`. The error is that `dlib` was compiled with support for Intel MKL, but it can't find the actual MKL library on your computer when it runs. You could either re-compile with MKL disabled (probably easiest) or make sure MKL is installed properly.

> Never mind, reading through the code, my understanding of the approach for setting cookie expiration was incorrect; you can set cookie expiration by a third optional argument to set_access_cookies and set_refresh_cookies

That isn't actually correct. That optional argument will change the `exp` time in the JWT itself, but it will not update the cookie expiration. IIRC the cookie expiration was set to 70 years in the future as it was a very simple way to fix session cookie settings. The thought behind it is even if your JWT is expired, it may be beneficial to still have the option to send that token to an endpoint, and receive a specific error message telling you that the token was expired, that can then be handled as needed on the frontend.

If this is causing problems for you I would totally be game to try and update this. My only concern is if people are currently looking for the `401 token expired` message instead when an access token expires while using cookies, and we change it to send back the `401 token not found` error message instead, it might break their app out from under them. I would like to make the update in a way that is backwords compatible with the current approach.

That means that the cookie named `access_token_cookie` was not present in the request. How are you creating the JWTs and passing them to the frontend? Are you remembering to use the ```set_access_cookies(resp, access_token)``` function to actually set the cookies when a user logs in?

Ah, yeah, you're right. The ordering is not guaranteed but we don't duplicate data.

```python
import tensorflow as tf
from threading import Thread, Lock
from itertools import cycle

class DataSet(object):
    def __init__(self, txt_files, thread_count, batch_size):
        self._x = tf.placeholder(tf.int32, [])
        self._example_queue = tf.FIFOQueue(shapes=[[]],
                                           dtypes=[tf.int32],
                                           capacity=2 * self._get_device_count() * batch_size)
        self._enqueue_op = self._example_queue.enqueue([self._x])
        self._txt_files = cycle(txt_files)
        self._batch_size = batch_size
        self._thread_count = thread_count

    def _get_device_count(self):
        return 4

    def start_queue_threads(self, session):
        batch_threads = [Thread(target=self._populate_batch_queue, args=(session,)) for i in xrange(self._thread_count)]
        for batch_thread in batch_threads:
            batch_thread.daemon = True
            batch_thread.start()

    def _populate_batch_queue(self, session):
        for source in self._txt_files:
            session.run(self._enqueue_op, feed_dict={self._x: source})

    def next_batch(self):
        source = self._example_queue.dequeue_many(self._batch_size)
        return source

    @property
    def total_batches(self):
        # Note: If len(_txt_files) % _batch_size != 0, this re-uses initial _txt_files
        return int(ceil(float(len(self._txt_files)) /float(self._batch_size)))

data = DataSet(range(50000), 8, 32)

s = tf.InteractiveSession()
s.run(tf.initialize_all_variables())
tf.train.start_queue_runners(sess=s)
data.start_queue_threads(s)

batch = data.next_batch()

for i in range(3):
    print("batch {}: {}".format(i, s.run(batch)))
```

JSON pickling is going to be slower than the binary protocol, that's the tradeoff. What's the surprise here? If you're really after the speed, you should use something like `msgpack`:

```python
>>> %timeit jsonpickle.encode(d)
10 loops, best of 3: 21.3 ms per loop

>>> %timeit cPickle.dumps(d)
100 loops, best of 3: 3.23 ms per loop

>>> %timeit msgpack.dumps(d)
100 loops, best of 3: 2.14 ms per loop
```

Hi @joaodlf. 

PyPika is just a SQL query builder, not an ORM like SQLAlchemy. Therefore, we don't provide functionality to connect to a database, you will have to construct the query yourself in PyPika, cast the query object to a string (to get the stringified query) and pass that to a database connection module.

We have some documentation on handling other database platforms: http://pypika.readthedocs.io/en/latest/3_advanced.html#handling-different-database-platforms

These different query classes are used to set some base settings that are different between the platforms, such as different quote characters. If you want to implement different functions specific to a platform, you can import the Function class and create your own subclass, like we do for some generic functions in the subclass module: http://pypika.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/pypika.functions.html?highlight=function.

If a SQL database platform you use is missing from the list of query classes, then feel free to create a PR implementing one.

Thanks!

One option you have, of course, is using the manual install/run steps from the [robotpy documentation](http://robotpy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/guide/running.html#on-the-robot-manual). It's not super convenient, but it works.

That option only works while you're ssh'ed into the robot, and isn't permanent. If you read [cli_deploy.py](https://github.com/robotpy/pyfrc/blob/master/lib/pyfrc/mains/cli_deploy.py), you can perform the same steps it does to deploy your code permanently. Here's what you need to do if you've deployed to that robot before: 

* ssh into the robot as `lvuser` (no password) and do `rm -rf /home/lvuser/py`
* From your host, execute `scp -r /path/to/robot/code lvuser@roborio-5045.local:/home/lvuser/py`

... and reset your robot, and that should do it. Certainly not as convenient, but you won't be dead in the water for your demo.

As you can see in the [load from keyring call](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/9d12348e26d8c143d0b675ed147094a9e54e1c97/setuptools/command/upload.py#L21-L29), you will be prompted for a password under a number of conditions:

1. The keyring module raises an error on import.
2. The password is not found.
3. The password resolves to blank.

Also, any other exception.

You should first double-check that your password is in the keyring. Do you see the password when you invoke `keyring get <url_for_repository> <username>`? Double-check that <url_for_repository> is an exact match for the URL as indicated in .pypirc or setup.cfg.

Failing that, you could use pdb to inspect your environment to better understand why the keyring is not resolving.

```
$ python -m pdb setup.py -q sdist upload
> /Users/jaraco/Dropbox/code/main/setuptools/setup.py(4)<module>()
-> """
(Pdb) break setuptools/command/upload.py:27
Breakpoint 1 at /Users/jaraco/Dropbox/code/main/setuptools/setuptools/command/upload.py:27
(Pdb) continue
warning: no files found matching '*' under directory 'setuptools/_vendor'
> /Users/jaraco/Dropbox/code/main/setuptools/setuptools/command/upload.py(27)_load_password_from_keyring()
-> return keyring.get_password(self.repository, self.username)
(Pdb) self.repository
'https://upload.pypi.org/legacy/'
(Pdb) self.username
'jaraco'
(Pdb) keyring.get_password(self.repository, self.username)
'<elided>'
(Pdb) quit
Password: 
```

If the code doesn't stop at line 27, you probably don't have a working keyring library installed. If `get_password` returns an empty string, it may not be configured for the backend you expect. Call `keyring.core.get_keyring()` to ensure that you're connecting to the backend you expect.

Hope that helps. Let us know what you find.

Thanks! A few suggestions

1. To copy, note that copy.deepcopy() can actually be finicky. I would suggest doing Workflow.from_dict(wflow.to_dict()). This serialization is guaranteed to be correct (if it isn't you will have other problems). If you think this is non-intuitive to most folks, I would then override the __copy__() and __deepcopy__() methods of Workflow so that anyone that tries to use the "standard" copy methods will get the correct result.

2. I would separate out the reset function of the copied workflow, i.e. add:

Workflow.reset(reset_ids=False) - this will reset the states and fw_states dict to WAITING. If the reset_ids is set to True, then it will also reset the ids.

3. Then Workflow.from_wflow() is just two lines of code - one that copies the old one, and one that resets the new one

Some additional musings:

- In theory, an item may be associated with more than one record.  Think book proceedings where each chapter lives as an independent bibilographic record.  In practice, does this happen, or would there always be a "parent record" for book?  Check with CERN library use case and other CC-ed libraries.

- Item borrowing times may differ for various items depending on their state.  This is akin to reference books never leaving the room.

- Item availability may differ depending on item location.  Think deposition store in the basement, or other building.  We need a "libraries" class as @aw-bib already commented on above.

   - It is good to study the current table structure to see what was deemed needed, such as `crcLIBRARY`.  Even if the code etc will be replaced fully, and even if the table structure will be changed fully.

- Borrowers are being sent recall email, if they are overdue.  This is another core feature that may need to be represented and made configurable.  Different libraries may have different workflows.

- In theory, borrowing times could differ for different class of users.  E.g. students, staff, professors, retirees.  In practice, does this have to happen?  Check with CERN library use case and other libraries.

- In Invenio 1, the acquisition and ILL were part of the module, so to speak. In Invenio 2, it will be good to separate them away.  Anyway, it seems profitable to discuss these use cases at the same time, so that the whole picture could be attacked globally.  Can you start thinking about and preparing sister RFCs for these?

- I like the state-machine-like approach with rebrushing our use cases from blank slate.  Instead of starting from scratch, have you got inspiration from existing state diagrams describing circulation processes and event diagrams and whatnot? This adds `flush` and `finish` methods to the `Compressor`
object in the extension module, renames the `compress` method to
`process`, and updates it to only process data. Now, one or more
`process` calls followed by a `finish` call will be equivalent
to a module-level `compress` call.

Note: To maximize the compression efficiency (and match
underlying Brotli behavior, the `Compressor` object `process`
method does not guarantee all input is immediately written to
output. To ensure immediate output, call `flush` to manually
flush the compression buffer. Extraneous flushing can increase
the size, but may be required when processing streaming data.

from connexion.exceptions import ExtraParameterProblem

def handle_bad_request():
    # Do what you like

app.add_error_handler(ExtraParameterProblem, handle_bad_request)

Je te joins le code la nouvelle classe qui fonctionne bien pour 'impot_revenu' dans lequel je teste la suppression des tranches actuelles, puis leur remplacement par des mêmes tranches identiques, ainsi que l'ajout d'une 6ème tranche :

class modify_impot_revenu_brackets(Reform):
    # A reform always defines an `apply` method that builds the reformed tax and benefit system from the reference one.
    # See https://doc.openfisca.fr/coding-the-legislation/reforms.html#writing-a-reform
    def apply(self):
        # Our reform modifies the 'impot_revenu' parameter, which is a scale.
        # This parameter is declared in `taxes.xml` using a `<BAREME>` XML element, the French name for "scale".
        #
        # See https://doc.openfisca.fr/coding-the-legislation/legislation_parameters.html
        self.modify_legislation_json(modifier_function=self.modify_brackets)
        
        # Suppression de variables
        self.neutralize_variable('af')
        self.neutralize_variable('cf')
        self.neutralize_variable('ars')
        self.neutralize_variable('rsa_socle')
        self.neutralize_variable('ppa')
              
        # Mise à jour de variables
        for variable in [rdb, rdb_enf, credit_impot_monoparentales,revenu_disponible]:
            self.update_variable(variable)
 
    def modify_brackets(self, reference_legislation_json_copy):
        # This function takes an argument `reference_legislation_json_copy` which is a JSON-like representation
        # of the XML element. It can be modified and must be returned.

        # Access the right legislation node:
        scale = reference_legislation_json_copy['children']['impot_revenu']['children']['bareme']
        brackets = scale['brackets']

        # Add 0.1 to the rates of the second bracket, keeping the same thresholds:
    #    for rate in brackets[1]['rate']:
    #        rate['value'] += 0.9

        # Remove the first bracket : pour modifier un taux, il est nécesaire de supprimer la tranche, puis de la recréer 
        del brackets[0]
        del brackets[1]
        del brackets[2]
        del brackets[3]
        del brackets[4]
                    
        # Add a new bracket:
        brackets.append({
            'rate': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0}],
            'threshold': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0}]
            })
        brackets.append({
            'rate': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0.14}],
            'threshold': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 9710}]
            })
        brackets.append({
            'rate': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0.3}],
            'threshold': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 26818}]
            })
        brackets.append({
            'rate': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0.41}],
            'threshold': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 71898}]
            })
        brackets.append({
            'rate': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0.45}],
            'threshold': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 152260}]
            })
        brackets.append({
            'rate': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 0.55}],
            'threshold': [{'start': '2016-01-01', 'value': 250000}]
            })

        return reference_legislation_json_copy

OK. I got some updates here. 
1. A hard cut below 0.5(actually, 0) is very easy. We can easily reverse calculate (x, y, z) to (ele, az gate) assuming radar is at (0,0,0) for each grid cell. Then we mask all gates whose elevations are below 0 or above 20 (or whatever reasonable maximum)

For 2 and 3, I simply propose replace RoI function as following: a gate has 2 x beam_width (or 3 x beam_width at high elevations) angular affects (in both elevation and azimuth, like a cone) and 2 * gate_size radial affects, The weight function is `0.005^(angular_diff) / dist^2`. This method is a combination of MRMS's method and IDW weighting, somehow producing higher results than n-nearest neighbor interpolation, but avoid any searches at all, keep the advantage of original mapping function.

You may be correct about a rename, but I'll say that the reason for the difference is that the Datatable class only knows about raw database values.  It's the Column's job to tidy up the value, optionally creating "pretty" versions.  Your suggestion about making them all ``default_value`` might be fine because it doesn't hurt this distinction.  Also, I think you're right about the documentation lacking an entry on the behavior.  It looks like I never got the relevant methods and attributes for Column listed in the docs.

On a side note (although I'd have to check to be sure) I think the processor functions get all of the mentioned values in ``kwargs``.  In a processor, it just so happens that ``default_value`` is usually the most practical raw value to work with.

If you run any of the tests individually they succeed, so I don't think that this is likely to affect end users with or without `pantsd`, since they will only have 1 live store per run.

But the unit test(s) appear to be 1) lazily dropping schedulers, 2) possibly not deallocating the `Store` when the scheduler is dropped. I'm going to attempt to fix forward rather than reverting... if I can't figure it out by end of day, I'll revert.

Hi @a7ypically 

I am not an expert but I will try to help.
Have you considered trying to use it in Asynchronous Mode (see https://python-xbee.readthedocs.io/en/latest/). I am not sure but it could be that the wait_read_frame() is too late and the reply message has already been sent? 

Maybe try this...
```
#! /usr/bin/python
import serial
from xbee import ZigBee
import pprint

# Open serial port and create xbee object
ser = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyUSB0', 9600)

# Called each time a packet is received - print packet
def receiveMessage(data):
    pp = pprint.PrettyPrinter(indent=4)
    pp.pprint(data)

# Called if there is an error - display error message
def xbeeError(error):
    print "XBee Error:", error

# Create ZigBee library API object, and spawn a listener thread
zb = ZigBee(ser, callback=receiveMessage, error_callback=xbeeError)

# Send packet
xbee.remote_at(dest_addr_long=b'', options=b'\x02', command=b'D4', parameter=b'\x04')

zb.halt()
ser.close()
```

You could also try the Synchonous Mode? with a loop around 'print xbee.wait_read_frame()'?

@shin- I have updated the PR. Please take a look.

Now instead of catching `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError` it makes `GET /containers/(id or name)/archive` to check non-existent `path` case. According to [Docker API](https://docs.docker.com/engine/api/v1.24/#3-endpoints) the response object in success case (i.e. `path` exists inside container) contains HTTP 200 status code and `X-Docker-Container-Path-Stat` header is set. If `path` doesn't exist inside a container the response object simply has 404 status code and mentioned header is not set. I have found out that checking just for proper status code is enough.

Passing response object from `GET /containers/(id or name)/archive` to `_raise_for_status` method allows to reproduce the exact `errors.NotFound` instance as it would be produced in scenario when transferring a small file (when everything works fine). 

I've also experimented with `HEAD /containers/(id or name)/archive` which could be a more "light-weight" implementation. The HEAD request has the same properties as GET in terms of status codes and `X-Docker-Container-Path-Stat` header behavior. However, passing the resulting response to `_raise_for_status` method doesn't allows to instantiate `errors.NotFound` instance with detalized error message like this:

```
docker.errors.NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found ("Could not find the file /foo in container ad1ca507a883de7dcea3a915bca9170cd6e1d3890fe1cec996618910f85781af")
```

Instead it returns this:

```
docker.errors.NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found
```

That's why I've decided to stick with GET.

That's why I want a --output-DIR parameter, which is applicable to single and multiple masters.

1. It should create the output folders unless they already exist.
2. It should only put the final binaries into it. The rest can be ignored by git and the build system. Bonus points for an option to not write any intermediary files to disk.

The problem is currently that the build system has to implement some logic to _find the actual directory with the final build artifacts_ in a input-file-agnostic way and that logic probably has be be revisited if fontmake decides to change output directories. In the same annoying way you had to reliably guess the filename of a generated binary (or replicate the logic by copy-pasting from the fontmake source) before fontmake decided to just use the input filename(?). If you have multiple processes building stuff, as you should, and writing to the same directory, tricks like ls'ing and looking at timestamps are a joke at best.

You must know source and target files beforehand and be able to easily and reliably guess what the output is and where, regardless of input. Everything else makes fontmake painful and error-prone to use in a build system context.

So, two comments:
Overall I really enjoyed reviewing this PR - going over commit by commit really made this initially daunting PR into a very structured experience. Also, technically speaking, the result is clearly more organised and better designed than the original code.

I did expect, however, to see the changes made to the logic so that I could understand the change in functionality (and compare that to the changes in the spec). Instead, I saw new files added with that functionality added from scratch - which made it effectively impossible to do that sort of comparison.

The problem with such rewrites is mostly from a maturity point of view - the previous code had already matured and all sort of edge cases were handled (and in a certain way). When rewriting (if not done in a very specific way), the maturity of the code 'resets' and the entire process needs to start over.
For example, now I can't be sure that the same edge cases are handled as well, and that the decisions for each edge case remain the same. 
These subtle changes might introduce new bugs, and could cause a ripple effect of other things breaking in whatever code that's using these libraries.

2nd comment is regarding percent:
I think that the 'divide by 100' behaviour should be tossed away from the spec as well :) , but as long as we're keeping it, it should be limited to `%％﹪٪` only and not the other `‰‱` signs, which need to be divided by 1,000 and 10,000 respectively.

conda installs its software under your installation prefix (or often in an env subfolder of that).

Touching files outside of that isn't something we *generally* want to do. Yes there's an app there but we do not register our app bundles with the system because you could have many copies of the same app bundle and it would be a mess.

It sounds to me, therefore, that the scenario we are playing with is a _current_ issue that ought to be created and tracked. What is changing here are simply the specific conditions under which it is exercised.

@msarahan, I love the idea of fixing things and contributing. But right now I'm trying to do my job, and conda is getting in the way rather than helping. I do not have these problems with `apt`.

Don't get me wrong. I love conda and I appreciate all the work people have done and continue to do to develop it. But I'm giving some feedback from the field that I feel is extremely important.

I stress that while most people are able to get most things to work right now, incompatibilities seem to be increasing, and soon to use conda will require so much experience and specific knowledge of channel incompatibilities, it will cease to be useful as a quick way to set up a computation environment, or more importantly, share reproducible computational environments.

Furthermore, at this point it seems like there are actually computational environments that _simply can't be created_ with conda. This is an even bigger problem. Suppose a lab is doing bioinformatics and shares a common software environment. Along comes a new package that improves their previous analysis. They install it and their entire environment breaks. This is entirely possible in the current state of affairs.

I don't know exactly what the solution is. Perhaps the conda algorithms need to be improved. Perhaps recipe submission guidelines need to be improved. Perhaps conda needs to go to a fully curated system.

My point is that for my current bioinformatics project, although I'm really only using a handful of packages that are available in the conda ecosystem, I can't create a working environment. From looking at posts and issues online, this problem is not just my own. This is not a good state of affairs.

When it was obvious that we had some performance issue and that storage drove a lot of specific questions, the storage team took ownership directly to improve it drastically and be more responsive. This implies that the "azure-storage" package is close to our package structure and use our meta-package, but is in another repo ([unlike DocDB for instance](https://github.com/Azure/azure-documentdb-python)).

On any request which is missing the cookie, the server's response clears the cookie stored in the browser. The comments indicate that this is to prevent unnecessary warnings:

https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/2aac713937c0487dd2193c85a69f02e9556421fe/notebook/auth/login.py#L178-L179

However, when the token is scope to a path (base_url), it's easy to make a request outside that scope which won't include the cookie. The server sees that unauthenticated request and erroneously deletes your cookie entirely.

Nice catch indeed, and I think the approach of keeping Agents in a dictionary seems like a good one, but there are some things to consider before adopting it:
* It will change the order in which Agents are called for step() in any models using the BaseScheduler's step() method. Currently they're called in the same order as they're added, but with this change they'll be called in an order that depends on the values of the Agents' unique_ids. This could be fixed by using an OrderedDict instead.
* More importantly, it will enforce the uniqueness of the unique_id each agent is created with. Currently, despite the name, nothing happens differently if unique_id is in fact not unique across the Agents of one's Model - indeed, the nature of each Agent's unique_id has no affect at all on the behavior of the model - so this change might break some existing models. I think there should at least be an exception raised if one tries to add an agent with a duplicate unique_id to a scheduler.

I think this raises interesting questions, though: why do Agents have unique_id at all? As far as I can tell, it's only currently used for logging. Is it useful to be able to specify the id, or should that be managed for the user?

> I understand why each worker needs the same items, but why is order important?

It is just how things work at the moment: after ensuring every worker collected the same node ids (and in the same order), the `master` node just send test *indexes* to each worker, hence order is important. We could change that so the `master` node sends node ids instead of indexes, but there might be performance implications that should be measured, specially when using remote workers (disclosure: my gut feeling is that performance should be negligible, but should certainly be measured).

Right now, the parser searches both... sites-enabled for what configuration directives are actually enabled and sites-available for all of the possible sites that you may have configured, for VirtualHost selection.

My idea was that Apache can automatically enable a site that may have been created, but not yet deployed yet.  If Apache is configured appropriately for Debian, sites-enabled should be a subset of sites-available.

The Apache VirtualHost objects have an attribute for if the site is currently enabled.

I do think that it makes sense to completely "generalize" the configuration by only searching sites-enabled (it would make porting to other layouts easier).

I would suggest that we don't make a distinction between sites-enabled and any other Included directory.  We should simply look through all of the current configuration files and look for VirtualHost blocks.

The other advantage of providing command line interface to obtain keys and certificate files is that it could be used for automatically distributing the pair to a cluster of HTTP servers.

In case of running a HTTP server cluster, once DV is complete, you would want to run the letsencrypt client on a single machine (to occasionally renew the certificate), and distribute the key and certificate file to the HTTP servers.

Without having a command line interface to obtain certificate files, it is impossible to setup such distribution unless you have the guts to write a plugin specific to your data-center needs.

But with a command line interface to obtain the files, it would become easy for administrators to write simple scripts to do the job.

In terms of code architecture it could be done very similar to what is done in this PR; we could simply spawn a new gzip process from within vmprof, get the pipe file descriptor and write to that (as opposed to writing to the output file directly). This doesn't require lots of changes in vmprof (only in the CLI module) and it's very convenient to use as we can integrate it with the CLI etc. Otherwise we could simply use the `-o` option to write to stdout and pipe that into gzip on the command line, but that wouldn't be as convenient to use from within Python because everyone would have to roll out their own gzip wiring. The way the formatters in `microcosm_logging` are intended to work are:

- There is a console logger which will format values from extra into the string specified in the log message
- There is a json logger which will *not* format values from extra into the log message instead it puts the values from extra in the json that is logged and maintains the format string as is so you can search by it.

Sacred by default sets up a root logger for you. So the duplication is from the two handlers (one from sacred one from you). 

You can either use sacreds logger and not create your own like this:
```python
from sacred import Experiment
from sacred.settings import SETTINGS
SETTINGS.CAPTURE_MODE = 'no'
ex = Experiment('test')

@ex.automain
def main(_log):
	_log.info("hahah")
```

or you can tell sacred to use your logger instead, by setting `ex.logger` like this:
``` python
import logging
from sacred import Experiment
from sacred.settings import SETTINGS
SETTINGS.CAPTURE_MODE = 'no'
ex = Experiment('test')

logger = logging.getLogger('test')
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
ch = logging.StreamHandler()
ch.setLevel(logging.INFO)
formatter = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')
ch.setFormatter(formatter)
logger.addHandler(ch)

ex.logger = logger  # <<== HERE

@ex.automain
def main():
	logger.info("hahah")
```
That satisfies my need for now, though of course it would be nice to have the option to use the same code automatically.

model.export_lib('gcc', libpath='./libtest.dylib', params={'parallel_comp':4}, nthread=4, verbose=True)

PROOF_MODE is deprecated. There used to be two flavors of proof tracing, a fine grained and a coarse grained mode. The coarse grained mode would summarize certain operations. The "fine" grained would attempt to track proof steps in greater detail. The coarse grained mode has been deprecated, so everything is "fine" grained, which for Z3 means a best-effort attempt. 

I have removed references to PROOF_MODE and updated the documentation.

Heres a partial example:
```Python
# gru_hid is the GRUCell which happens to also be the last layer in the 
# network used inside scan
def setup_model(deterministic):
    X_to_scan = lasagne.layers.get_output(x_to_recurrent_network, {x_in: sym_x}, deterministic=deterministic)
    hid_init = gru_hid.get_hid_init(num_batch=batch_size)
    recurrence_params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(gru_hid)
    def recurrence(x_t, hid_previous, *args):
        hid_t = lasagne.layers.get_output(
            gru_hid,
            {x_recurrent_in: x_t,
             hid_previous_in: hid_previous})
        return hid_t

    hid_out, rnn_updates = theano.scan(
        fn=recurrence,
        sequences=[X_to_scan],
        outputs_info=[hid_init], 
        non_sequences=recurrence_params,
        strict=True)
    # get random variables to update....
    for k, v in rnn_updates.iteritems():
        k.default_update = v

    return hid_out
```
You need to be carefull to not get the shapes wrong. 

``x_to_recurrent_network``: has output shape ``[seq_len, batch_size, num_inputs]``.

``gru_hid`` has input shape ``[batch_size, num_inputs]`` and output shape ``[batch_size, num_hidden_gru]``.

If you put a network after scan it would have to have input shape ``[seq_len, batch_size, num_hidden_gru]``.
Anything with an upper-casing will be auto-escaped because postgres didn't believe in uppercase. If that's not a problem in mssql then there is no need for this escaping.

Hi Will,

Thank you for suggestions.

That will work in the first example. But there are other use cases, for example, our application allows user to download multiple files in bulk as a ZIP archive. It receives a list of file names in request and packs them into one ZIP file on flight during streaming to the web browser (i.e. no intermediary temporary file is used). The files potentially can reside on different file systems (one local, another on SFTP server, third on S3). In this case, we need to worry about closing multiple file system objects.

I don't say that it is not possible to create a solution for this case as well.
I just say, that FileSystems2 is supposed to provide abstraction layer to remote file systems and behave just like local file system (open file object, close it and do not worry about closing the file system itself). The user should expect to open a file from remote file system and pass this file-like object to any other parts of application without wrapping it into additional layer of abstraction.

Best regards,
Gennadiy

Before broadening the scope of this too much and adding new things on the pile, let's go back to the *accounts API* feature.

The code of this PR was ready. I was thinking that we could rewrite the whole project documentation to rely on it instead of basic auth. And that last part never happened because it represents a lot of documentation effort. 

From here, we can imagine several strategies (*add yours!*):
* Merge it as an hidden/experimental feature (ie. just code, no docs)
* Merge it and document it as an extra feature (ie. without rewriting every docs)
* Merge it once the whole documentation is ready (ie. requires lots of efforts)
* Move it to a separate repo for less friction and keep the core repo as small as possible
* Drop this PR (which has hackish parts) and rely on a more solid solution like Portier by default (see #994)

There have been two PRs attempting this: #3369 and #3687.

#3369 was a very small fix, which set, in the server classes:

`address_family = socket.AF_INET6`

It had two problems: It was failing unittests, and it wouldn't work on (a) OS's that don't support IPv6 (note: different from instances that lack an IPv6 interface), and (b) Python versions compiled without IPv6.

I am still hopeful that such an approach could work. For instance, could we wrap the assignment to address_family in a `try/except` that just `pass`es if there's an exception? The main blocker is that I don't know how to set up an OS without IPv6 support. I think doing that would be an important first step if someone wants to pick up this work.

On Fri, 04 Nov 2016 13:12:17 -0700, Peter Eckersley <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> @bitdancer I see you wontfixed [the argparse
> bug](https://bugs.python.org/issue27153) for the case of `store_false`
> flags. I think you might have made the wrong decision there, because
> you're reasoning about the default value of an internal Python
> variable, whereas a user reading commandline help has no idea about
> Python variables, and is thinking about the default for whether a
> *flag* is in effect or not.

Yes, that was exactly my point.  What does

    --no-foo  Suppress foo (default: False)

mean?  It could mean that foo is off by default.  You just can't tell,
given only the phrase 'suppress foo' as the description of the flag.  So
it wouldn't help to flip the value of the default, the sentence is
ambiguous either way.  What you need to do is write the sentence so
that it is not ambiguous, if you can.  But I'm not sure you can,
because of the inherent nonsensicality of mentioning the default
value of a variable that is, as you say, hidden from the user.

> But a general question: is there a sane way to stop `argparse` from
> printing an incorrect default for a few flags, but keep it printing
> defaults for all the other ones? That would clearly be useful for
> projects like this one that have dozens or hundreds of flags, most but
> not all of which are correctly documented by `argparse`.

Not currently.  Enhancement patches welcome :)

I've solved this issue this way in my cityscape dataset wrapper:
```
def __getitem__(self,index):      
        img = Image.open(self.data[index]).convert('RGB')
        target = Image.open(self.data_labels[index])
        
        seed = np.random.randint(2147483647) # make a seed with numpy generator 
        random.seed(seed) # apply this seed to img tranfsorms
        if self.transform is not None:
            img = self.transform(img)
            
        random.seed(seed) # apply this seed to target tranfsorms
        if self.target_transform is not None:
            target = self.target_transform(target)

        target = torch.ByteTensor(np.array(target))
    
        return img, target
```

Just to carry over the most important bit I care about from slack discussion, I think it's very important to preserve the `.remote() <=> future` invariant. It means that `.remote()` is exactly defined as returning a future, no more, no less. In other words, all futures that can be returned, should be returned through `remote()` and `remote()` can only return a future. If we fail to preserve this property, I foresee confusion developers might have. We don't want them to mentally differentiate between `.remote()` being used for functions and methods and `.remote()` being used for `ray.class` instantiation. It is very convenient to know that I can always call `ray.get` and ray.wait` on the result of any call to `.remote()`. Failing that breaks the composability of our API. For those who are familiar with basic principles of PL, I'm talking about the orthogonality of our API here:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orthogonality_(programming)

Come to think of it, this solution (of using my python script to edit the `/etc/fstab` to increase shared memory) seems very convoluted -- I mean surely, I'm not the first to encounter this problem of having to run ray on a remote machine. 

So I think I may be going about solving my problem the wrong way. I have a collection of (say) 30 files (in AWS/S3) that need to be read by my training class. I essentially have a loop:

```
for i in range(30):
   dataframe <- read file i (~ 20 secs)
   process dataframe (~ 30 secs)
```

Instead of doing this sequentially, I was hoping to leverage ray to asynchronously launch a collection `file_read()` jobs, and have a loop where after processing a dataframe, I grab the next "ready" dataframe (which would have been fetched while I was processing):

```
df_ids <- launch_readers()
for i in range(30):
    ready_id = get_next_ready_id()  # fast after the first one
    df <- ray.get(ready_id)  # fast
    process df    # ~ 30 secs
```

I first thought I would try to have a global cache (dict) that maps a file-index to the data-frame, and each file-reading task would add an entry to the dict: i -> data_frame_i, and the read task would simply return an index to the cache, rather than the actual data-frame, thus side-stepping the whole issue of shared memory size, serializability, etc.
But then this would need the tasks to be stateful, so it wouldn't work. I then thought about making my main class a remote class (actor), but I didn't like that because every method invocation (there are many) on that class would need to be made remote.

So is there some way I can maintain a global cache dict of index -> dataFrame, and use ray to asynchronously read files into data-frames and add them to the cache? Or some other paradigm I haven't thought of?

I agree that the quality of new issues has decreased. I went and looked back at some old issues, and it seemed like there were still a large number of bad issues. I think the main problem is that in the past we would let bad issues slip by because we only had the one template. Now that we have multiple templates our expectations around issues have gone up which is making these low effort issues more prevalent.

****Is the template list too confusing?***

I'm not sure either. It seems to me that people simply are not reading the templates (or even the titles which is crazy to me). We can modify the order but we would have to prefix the titles with a numerical value or some other kind of identifier to force the order. Regarding descriptions, I'm not sure what we can be more clear on here. They are pretty concise and to the point, and if people aren't even reading the template titles, what will make them read longer descriptions? I'm open to trying any of these ideas however.

***Are the individual templates too complicated and overwhelming?***

I'd be fine with removing more content. It's worth noting that a fair amount of notation was removed/moved when the templates were recreated to try and help with this as people still weren't reading. If we can cut more let's try it, worst case we revert. It could be the HTML tags? I'm really not sure. I agree the first comment block is pretty large and people are ignoring it, but I'm not sure how else we get them to actually read the contributing docs. We specifically note to look at the preview option.

I agree it's not a dramatic issue, but it annoys me every single day and I hate wasting time on these issues. Depending on what is discussed here I would actually propose a more aggressive method:

- Create example issues (which are closed) of each template and link those. We add these to each template and the description, as well as the contributing documentation.
- A new tag is created along the lines of `does not meet issue requirements`, and we create a few default GitHub replies that link to the docs as well as the example template.
- We close and lock any issue that doesn't meet the requirements and paste in the reply.

I'm getting pretty sick of repeated low effort issues and if people can't learn, or aren't willing to take the time to figure out how to create an issue asking us for free work then it isn't worth our time. It doesn't help that we've now got a lot of major sites covered with Streamlink, so many of the requests/issues we're seeing are for smaller sites, or weird configurations.

You can try this code below, modified from the mnist example. It is based on my understanding of what you're doing, and this code works as I expected:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import os
import argparse
import numpy as np



# Just import everything into current namespace
from tensorpack import *
from tensorpack.tfutils import summary
from tensorpack.dataflow import dataset

IMAGE_SIZE = 28


class Model(ModelDesc):
    def inputs(self):
        """
        Define all the inputs (with type, shape, name) that the graph will need.
        """
        return [tf.placeholder(tf.float32, (None, IMAGE_SIZE, IMAGE_SIZE), 'input'),
                tf.placeholder(tf.int32, (None,), 'label')]

    def build_graph(self, image, label):
        """This function should build the model which takes the input variables
        and return cost at the end"""

        # In tensorflow, inputs to convolution function are assumed to be
        # NHWC. Add a single channel here.
        image = tf.expand_dims(image, 3)

        image = image * 2 - 1   # center the pixels values at zero
        # The context manager `argscope` sets the default option for all the layers under
        # this context. Here we use 32 channel convolution with shape 3x3
        with argscope(Conv2D, kernel_size=3, activation=tf.nn.relu, filters=32):
            logits = (LinearWrap(image)
                      .Conv2D('conv0')
                      .MaxPooling('pool0', 2)
                      .Conv2D('conv1')
                      .Conv2D('conv2')
                      .MaxPooling('pool1', 2)
                      .Conv2D('conv3')
                      .FullyConnected('fc0', 512, activation=tf.nn.relu)
                      .Dropout('dropout', rate=0.5)
                      .FullyConnected('fc1', 10, activation=tf.identity)())

        tf.nn.softmax(logits, name='prob')   # a Bx10 with probabilities

        # a vector of length B with loss of each sample
        cost = tf.nn.sparse_softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits=logits, labels=label)
        cost = tf.reduce_mean(cost, name='cross_entropy_loss')  # the average cross-entropy loss

        return cost

    def optimizer(self):
        return tf.train.AdamOptimizer(0.01)



def get_config():
    def gen():
        cnt = 0
        while True:
            cnt += 1
            print('------------', cnt)
            for k in range(1024):
                yield [np.random.rand(28, 28), 0]
    dataset_train = DataFromGenerator(gen)
    steps_per_epoch = 10
    return TrainConfig(
        model=Model(),
        data=BatchQueueInput(dataset_train, 1024),  # the DataFlow instance for training
        callbacks=[ ],
        steps_per_epoch=steps_per_epoch,
        max_epoch=100,
    )


if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--gpu', help='comma separated list of GPU(s) to use.')
    parser.add_argument('--load', help='load model')
    args = parser.parse_args()
    if args.gpu:
        os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = args.gpu

    # automatically setup the directory train_log/mnist-convnet for logging
    logger.auto_set_dir()

    config = get_config()
    if args.load:
        config.session_init = SaverRestore(args.load)
    # SimpleTrainer is slow, this is just a demo.
    # You can use QueueInputTrainer instead
    launch_train_with_config(config, SimpleTrainer())
```

The way I'd like to do it would be roughly like:

```yaml
services:
- docker

# TODO: add compatibility matrix
before_install:
- docker build --rm -t tensorlayer:snapshot -f docker/Dockerfile.test .

script:
- docker run --rm -it tensorlayer:snapshot pytest
```

* enable [docker service](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/docker/) in travis.yml 
* build a temporary docker image on travis CI
* run tests inside the temporary image

Then you will be able to reproduce what exactly happened on travis-ci by typing the following commands:

```bash
docker build --rm -t tensorlayer:snapshot -f docker/Dockerfile.test .
docker run --rm -it tensorlayer:snapshot pytest
```

I think this way is more hermetic, and portable. (This is exactly what docker tries to solve.)
